summary: I was born in Hargeisa, Somalia in 1980 and experienced the hardships of the Somali civil war firsthand. As a child, I was injured during a government artillery barrage, which forced my family and me to flee to Ethiopia as refugees. Later, I earned a scholarship to study Islamic law at Al-Azhar University in Egypt, where I met my wife, Halimo. In 2012, we moved to the United States to build a better life, and I am currently studying Contemporary History at Saint Paul College.

Hargeisa is where I was born in 1980, a city in northern Somalia. It was a place marked by intense poverty and violence, especially starting in 1988 when unrest broke out. My childhood there was deeply affected by the conflict, including a government artillery attack that injured me and forced my family to flee. Despite its challenges, Hargeisa remains the starting point of my journey.

Somalia is my homeland, but it was torn apart by violent unrest starting in 1988 and the civil war that broke out in 1991. The collapse of the government led to widespread devastation, including the destruction of infrastructure and education systems. My family and I were forced to flee as refugees due to the violence, and the trauma of these events shaped much of my early life and my essay that won me a scholarship to study abroad.

During the Somali civil war, my family and I fled to Ethiopia as refugees to escape the violence in Somalia. We stayed in a refugee camp there before eventually returning to Somalia, which had been devastated by the conflict. Ethiopia was a temporary but crucial refuge during those difficult years of my childhood.

The Somali Civil War began in 1991 after the collapse of the Somali government and was a brutal conflict involving various rebel factions. It caused massive destruction to the country’s infrastructure and forced hundreds of thousands, including my family, to become refugees. The war deeply affected my childhood, and I wrote an essay about these traumatic memories that helped me win a scholarship to study at Al-Azhar University.

In 2003, I was awarded a scholarship to study Islamic law at Al-Azhar University in Egypt after writing an essay about my experiences during the Somali civil war. Studying there was a turning point in my life, as it allowed me to pursue higher education despite the devastation back home. It was also where I met my wife, Halimo, which eventually led to our move to the United States in 2012.

My wife, Halimo, had already been living in the United States before I joined her in 2012. We moved here together to seek better opportunities and build a new life. I am currently a student at Saint Paul College in the Twin Cities, part of a growing East African community, and I continue to pursue my education and contribute to this community.

Halimo is my wife and a fellow Somali immigrant who had been living in the United States before I arrived. We met while I was studying at Al-Azhar University in Egypt, and she played a key role in helping me come to the U.S. in 2012. We share a strong bond through our shared heritage and immigrant experiences.

I am currently a student at Saint Paul College, where I study Contemporary History. The college is located near a large East African community in the Twin Cities, which has been a supportive environment for me as an immigrant. My studies here are part of my ongoing journey to build a better life in the United States.

The Twin Cities area is where I live now, surrounded by a growing East African population. It has become home for many immigrants like me, offering a community where we can support each other. Living here while attending Saint Paul College has helped me feel connected and hopeful about the future.
